subject: Mathematics
chapter: Chapter 1: A Square and a Cube

key_points: A number is a perfect square if its prime factorisation contains even powers of all prime factors
A number is a perfect cube if its prime factorisation contains powers that are multiples of three
Square roots and cube roots can be found using prime factorisation or estimation methods
Perfect squares always have an odd number of factors
Perfect cubes maintain the sign of the original number
examples: 144 = 2^4 × 3^2 → perfect square because all powers are even
216 = 2^3 × 3^3 → perfect cube because powers are multiples of 3
formulas: Square root using prime factorisation → group primes in pairs
Cube root using prime factorisation → group primes in triples
exam_tips: Check prime factorisation carefully when identifying perfect squares or cubes
Remember that square numbers end with digits 0,1,4,5,6 or 9
Cube numbers can end with any digit but follow repeating patterns
Practice estimation techniques to quickly approximate square roots
